title: Senate Elections
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'An Act to alter the Constitution so as to provide for the election of Senators for each State by the people of the State'?
content: This referendum altered the Senate election timetable so that Senate terms began on 1 July rather than 1 January, resolving practical difficulties with the original constitutional provision. It was the first successful referendum and passed with strong support in all States except Queensland.

title: Finance
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'An Act to alter the Constitution relating to the Finance of the Commonwealth'?
content: This proposal sought to alter the financial provisions of the Constitution relating to the distribution of surplus revenue between the Commonwealth and the States. Although it received a slim majority of votes nationally, it failed to achieve a majority in four of the six States.

title: State Debts
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'An Act to alter the Constitution so as to provide for the taking over of State Debts'?
content: This referendum amended s 105 of the Constitution to allow the Commonwealth to take over the public debts of the States existing at the time of federation. It passed with a national majority and majorities in all States except New South Wales.

title: Finance
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'An Act to alter the Constitution relating to the Finance of the Commonwealth'?
content: This proposal sought to repeal s 87 (the Braddon clause) which required the Commonwealth to return three-quarters of customs and excise revenue to the States. It was defeated, failing to achieve both a national majority and a majority of States.

title: Legislative Powers
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Legislative Powers) 1910'?
content: This omnibus proposal sought to greatly expand Commonwealth legislative power over trade and commerce, corporations, industrial matters, railway disputes, and other subjects. It was defeated, with only New South Wales and Queensland voting in favour.

title: Monopolies
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Monopolies) 1910'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power to declare monopolies unlawful and to nationalise them on just terms. It was defeated alongside the legislative powers proposal, reflecting public resistance to a major expansion of Commonwealth power.

title: Trade and Commerce
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Trade and Commerce) 1912'?
content: The Fisher Labor government re-submitted the 1911 trade and commerce proposal, seeking to expand Commonwealth power over all trade and commerce. It was defeated again with a nearly identical result.

title: Corporations
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Corporations) 1912'?
content: This proposal sought to expand the corporations power to give the Commonwealth full regulatory power over foreign and trading corporations. It was defeated alongside the other 1913 proposals.

title: Industrial Matters
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Industrial Matters) 1912'?
content: The 1911 industrial matters proposal was resubmitted and again defeated, reflecting ongoing resistance to extending Commonwealth industrial relations powers.

title: Railway Disputes
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Railway Disputes) 1912'?
content: The railway disputes proposal was resubmitted and again defeated. All six 1913 proposals failed, mirroring the 1911 results.

title: Trusts
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Trusts) 1912'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power over trusts, combinations, and monopolies in restraint of trade, including the power to nationalise monopolies on just terms. It was defeated alongside all other 1913 proposals, confirming the electorate's unwillingness to grant the Commonwealth such extensive economic powers.

title: Legislative Powers
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Legislative Powers) 1915'?
content: This proposal sought to make permanent various Commonwealth powers that had been temporarily conferred during World War I, including powers over trade and commerce, corporations, and industrial matters. It was narrowly defeated nationally and failed to achieve a majority of States.

title: Nationalisation of Monopolies
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Nationalisation of Monopolies) 1915'?
content: The third attempt at a nationalisation of monopolies referendum was again defeated, continuing the pattern of failed attempts to expand Commonwealth economic powers.

title: Industry and Commerce
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Industry and Commerce) 1926'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power to make laws with respect to corporations, trusts, and industrial matters, consolidating previous defeated proposals. It was comfortably defeated.

title: Essential Services
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Essential Services) 1926'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth temporary power to protect essential services during industrial disputes. It was defeated, reflecting continuing public reluctance to expand Commonwealth industrial powers.

title: State Debts
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (State Debts) 1928'?
content: This referendum inserted s 105A into the Constitution, providing constitutional backing for the Financial Agreement between the Commonwealth and the States establishing the Australian Loan Council. It passed with a very strong majority in all six States, reflecting bipartisan support for cooperative financial arrangements.

title: Aviation
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Aviation) 1936'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth express power over aviation and air navigation. Despite achieving a national majority of votes, it failed because it did not achieve a majority in four or more States.

title: Marketing
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Marketing) 1937'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power over the marketing of primary products. Although it achieved a slim national majority, it failed to obtain a majority of States.

title: Post-War Reconstruction and Democratic Rights
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Post-War Reconstruction and Democratic Rights) 1944'?
content: This single omnibus proposal sought to grant the Commonwealth 14 additional powers for a five-year post-war reconstruction period, covering employment, marketing, corporations, trusts, profiteering, and other matters. Despite wartime conditions, it was defeated, demonstrating the difficulty of passing broadly framed constitutional amendments.

title: Social Services
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Social Services) 1946'?
content: This referendum inserted s 51(xxiiiA) into the Constitution, giving the Commonwealth express power to provide social services including maternity allowances, widows' pensions, child endowment, unemployment benefits, pharmaceutical benefits, sickness and hospital benefits, medical and dental services, and student benefits. The provision also included a guarantee of civil conscription protection for medical practitioners.

title: Organised Marketing of Primary Products
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Organised Marketing of Primary Products) 1946'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power over the organised marketing of primary products. While it achieved a slim national majority of votes, it failed because only three States voted in favour, not the required four.

title: Industrial Employment
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Industrial Employment) 1946'?
content: This proposal sought to expand the Commonwealth's industrial powers to include terms and conditions of industrial employment. It achieved a narrow national majority but failed to secure a majority of States.

title: Rents and Prices
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Rents and Prices) 1947'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power to control rents and prices. It was comprehensively defeated, with no State voting in favour, reflecting strong opposition to Commonwealth intervention in economic matters during peacetime.

title: Powers to deal with Communists and Communism
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Powers to deal with Communists and Communism) 1951'?
content: Following the High Court's decision striking down the Communist Party Dissolution Act, the Menzies government sought a constitutional amendment to authorise the banning of the Communist Party. The referendum was narrowly defeated, with three States in favour and three against, and a slim national majority voting No.

title: Aboriginal People
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'An Act to alter the Constitution so as to omit certain words relating to the People of the Aboriginal Race in any State and so that Aboriginals are to be counted in reckoning the Population'?
content: This referendum achieved the highest Yes vote in Australian referendum history, removing the words 'other than the aboriginal race in any State' from s 51(xxvi) (the races power), allowing the Commonwealth to make laws for Aboriginal people, and repealing s 127 which excluded Aboriginal people from population counts. It passed with overwhelming support in all six States.

title: Parliament (Nexus)
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Parliament) 1967'?
content: This proposal sought to break the nexus between the size of the Senate and the House of Representatives required by s 24, which mandates the House be 'as nearly as practicable' twice the size of the Senate. It was soundly defeated despite being put to the vote on the same day as the highly successful Aboriginal people referendum.

title: Prices
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Prices) 1973'?
content: The Whitlam government proposed giving the Commonwealth power to control prices. It was defeated in all six States, continuing the pattern of failed attempts to extend Commonwealth economic regulatory power.

title: Incomes
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Incomes) 1973'?
content: This proposal sought to give the Commonwealth power to regulate incomes. It was heavily defeated in all six States, reflecting broad opposition to Commonwealth wage and income controls.

title: Simultaneous Elections
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Simultaneous Elections) 1974'?
content: This proposal sought to ensure that Senate and House of Representatives elections would be held simultaneously. Although it received significant support, it failed to achieve a national majority or a majority of States.

title: Mode of Altering the Constitution
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Mode of Altering the Constitution) 1974'?
content: This proposal sought to make it easier to amend the Constitution by allowing referendums to be carried by a national majority alone, without the requirement for a majority of States. It was defeated in all six States.

title: Democratic Elections
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Democratic Elections) 1974'?
content: This proposal sought to require all electoral divisions to contain equal numbers of electors, applying the principle of one vote one value. It was defeated despite the Whitlam government's strong advocacy for electoral reform.

title: Local Government Bodies
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Local Government Bodies) 1974'?
content: This proposal sought to allow the Commonwealth to borrow money for and grant financial assistance directly to local government bodies. It was defeated in all six States, failing to secure direct constitutional recognition for local government.

title: Simultaneous Elections
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Simultaneous Elections) 1977'?
content: The simultaneous elections proposal was resubmitted by the Fraser government. Despite receiving a strong 62% national Yes vote, it was defeated because only two States (New South Wales and Victoria) voted in favour, failing to meet the double majority requirement.

title: Senate Casual Vacancies
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Senate Casual Vacancies) 1977'?
content: This referendum amended s 15 of the Constitution to require that a casual Senate vacancy be filled by a person from the same political party as the departing senator, prompted by controversial appointments following the 1975 constitutional crisis. It passed overwhelmingly in all six States.

title: Referendums (Territories)
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Referendums) 1977'?
content: This referendum amended s 128 to allow residents of the Australian Capital Territory and the Northern Territory to vote in constitutional referendums. It passed with strong support in all six States.

title: Retirement of Judges
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Retirement of Judges) 1977'?
content: This referendum amended s 72 of the Constitution to provide for a mandatory retirement age of 70 for federal judges, replacing the previous provision for life tenure. It passed with the support of over 80% of voters and all six States.

title: Interchange of Powers
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Interchange of Powers) 1984'?
content: This proposal sought to enable the Commonwealth and the States to refer legislative powers to each other. It was defeated, reflecting concerns about the potential for such arrangements to undermine the federal balance.

title: Terms of Senators
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Terms of Senators) 1984'?
content: This proposal sought to link Senate terms to House of Representatives elections, effectively achieving simultaneous elections. Although it achieved a slim national majority of votes, it failed because only two States voted in favour.

title: Parliamentary Terms
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Parliamentary Terms) 1988'?
content: This proposal sought to extend the maximum term of the House of Representatives from three to four years. It was heavily defeated in all six States, reflecting public distrust of politicians extending their own terms.

title: Fair Elections
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Fair Elections) 1988'?
content: This proposal sought to enshrine the principle of one vote one value in the Constitution by requiring electoral divisions to contain equal numbers of people. It was defeated in all six States.

title: Local Government
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Local Government) 1988'?
content: This second attempt to achieve constitutional recognition for local government was heavily defeated in all six States. The proposal sought to insert a provision recognising the existence of local government.

title: Rights and Freedoms
question: Do you approve of the proposed law for the alteration of the Constitution entitled 'Constitution Alteration (Rights and Freedoms) 1988'?
content: This proposal sought to extend the existing constitutional rights provisions, including extending the right to trial by jury, freedom of religion, and fair terms for compulsory acquisition to apply against State governments. It received the lowest Yes vote of the 1988 referendums and was defeated in all six States.

title: Republic
question: Do you approve of the proposed law to alter the Constitution to establish the Commonwealth of Australia as a republic with the Queen and Governor-General being replaced by a President appointed by a two-thirds majority of the members of the Commonwealth Parliament?
content: The republic referendum proposed replacing the Queen and Governor-General with a President appointed by a two-thirds majority of both houses of Parliament. It was defeated in all six States, with republicans divided over the appointment model and monarchists campaigning effectively against the specific proposal.

title: Preamble
question: Do you approve of the proposed law to alter the Constitution to insert a preamble?
content: This proposal sought to insert a new preamble into the Constitution acknowledging Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples as the nation's first people and other matters. It was defeated in all six States, with criticism of the proposed wording coming from both supporters and opponents of a new preamble.
